does: This paper challenges the viability of using AI agents to automate alignment research as a strategy for achieving superintelligence alignment. Rather than focusing on intentional deception or scheming, the authors identify a more fundamental problem: alignment research inherently involves fuzzy, hard-to-supervise tasks lacking clear evaluation criteria. The core claim is that AI agents performing such research will systematically produce undetected errors that, when aggregated, generate misleadingly confident but catastrophically wrong safety assessments. The paper argues this failure mode is worse for automated than human research because optimization pressures concentrate agent errors in areas humans are least equipped to catch, and agent mistakes will have unusual error signatures that evade human detection patterns.
connects: This work builds on longstanding concerns about AI safety and the feasibility of different alignment strategies, particularly responding to recent proposals that automated alignment research could safely scale alignment efforts alongside AI capabilities. It extends prior work on the problem of evaluating AI systems' work quality, specification gaming, and how optimization pressure creates unexpected failure modes. The paper engages with debates about whether superhuman AI systems can safely oversee complex human-like tasks, drawing on research into scalable oversight and the limits of human evaluation. It also connects to work on systematic biases in human judgment and how verification scales poorly for fuzzy tasks, while responding to the implicit assumption in recursive alignment proposals that alignment research is amenable to the same automation approaches as other technical work.
enables: This analysis points toward several future research directions: developing better methods for evaluating and supervising fuzzy research tasks that resist adversarial optimization, understanding what properties of research tasks make them automatable versus inherently requiring human judgment, and designing safety mechanisms for scenarios where we must employ AI to work on safety itself. The work motivates investigation into error detection and discovery methods that can identify non-human-like mistake patterns. It also raises questions about what forms of alignment research might be safer to automate and which certification or verification procedures could maintain safety guarantees when oversight is imperfect. More broadly, it suggests the need for alignment strategies that do not depend on automating alignment research itself, potentially informing the feasibility assessment of different paths to superintelligence safety.
